skill.name: k8s-security-policies
skill.description: Implement Kubernetes security policies including NetworkPolicy, PodSecurityPolicy, and RBAC for production-grade security
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for implementing Kubernetes network policies, pod security standards, and RBAC configurations
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analyzer findings are false positives. The skill contains documentation and YAML templates for Kubernetes security configurations. Network pattern detections reference metadata endpoint blocking (security best practice), and external command findings are bash examples in Markdown documentation, not executable code.

evidence.context: IP 169.254.169.254 is blocked in except list - this is a security hardening measure
Markdown documentation with kubectl command examples in code blocks
Markdown documentation with kubectl command examples in code blocks

content.value_statement: Secure your Kubernetes clusters with production-ready network policies, RBAC configurations, and pod security standards. This skill provides comprehensive templates and best practices for defense-in-depth security.
actual_capabilities: Generate NetworkPolicy manifests for network segmentation and traffic control
Create RBAC Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ClusterRoleBindings
Configure Pod Security Standards at namespace level (privileged, baseline, restricted)
Set up OPA Gatekeeper constraint templates for policy enforcement
Configure Istio PeerAuthentication and AuthorizationPolicy for service mesh security
Provide kubectl commands for troubleshooting security configurations
limitations: Requires Kubernetes cluster access to apply generated manifests
NetworkPolicy enforcement depends on CNI plugin support (Calico, Cilium, etc.)
Pod Security Policies are deprecated in Kubernetes 1.21+, use Pod Security Standards instead
Service mesh features require Istio installation

use_cases.title: DevSecOps Engineer
use_cases.description: Implement network isolation between microservices and enforce least-privilege access controls for CI/CD pipelines

use_cases.title: Platform Team Lead
use_cases.description: Establish security baselines and compliance controls across multi-tenant Kubernetes clusters
use_cases.target_user: Platform engineering teams managing shared clusters

use_cases.title: Security Auditor
use_cases.description: Review and validate existing Kubernetes security configurations against CIS benchmarks and NIST frameworks

prompt_templates.title: Basic Network Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a NetworkPolicy that allows frontend pods to communicate with backend pods on port 8080 in the production namespace
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing basic network segmentation between application tiers

prompt_templates.title: RBAC for Service Account
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate RBAC configuration for a service account that needs read-only access to ConfigMaps in the default namespace
prompt_templates.scenario: Granting minimal permissions to application service accounts

prompt_templates.title: Pod Security Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure namespace labels to enforce restricted Pod Security Standards with audit and warn modes
prompt_templates.scenario: Enforcing security baselines at namespace level

prompt_templates.title: Complete Security Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a comprehensive security configuration including default deny NetworkPolicy, RBAC for developers, and Pod Security Standards for a new production namespace
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up security for a new environment from scratch

output_examples.input: Create a default deny NetworkPolicy for the production namespace
output_examples.output: A complete NetworkPolicy manifest with empty podSelector and both Ingress and Egress policy types, blocking all traffic by default

output_examples.input: Generate RBAC for a deployment manager role
output_examples.output: A Role with permissions for deployments (full CRUD) and pods (read-only), plus a RoleBinding to attach users or service accounts

best_practices: Start with default deny NetworkPolicies, then explicitly allow required traffic
Apply least-privilege principle for RBAC - grant minimum permissions needed
Use dedicated ServiceAccounts for each application instead of default ServiceAccount
anti_patterns: Using wildcard (*) permissions in RBAC for production workloads
Running containers as root or with privileged security context
Allowing unrestricted egress traffic without explicit NetworkPolicy rules

faq.question: What CNI plugins support NetworkPolicy?
faq.answer: Popular CNIs with NetworkPolicy support include Calico, Cilium, Weave Net, and Antrea. Check your cluster documentation for compatibility.

faq.question: How do I test NetworkPolicy changes safely?
faq.answer: Apply policies in audit or warn mode first, use kubectl auth can-i for RBAC testing, and test in non-production environments before deploying.

faq.question: What is the difference between Role and ClusterRole?
faq.answer: Roles are namespace-scoped and grant permissions within a single namespace. ClusterRoles are cluster-wide and can grant permissions across all namespaces.

faq.question: Are Pod Security Policies still available?
faq.answer: Pod Security Policies are deprecated since Kubernetes 1.21 and removed in 1.25. Use Pod Security Standards with namespace labels instead.

faq.question: How do I allow DNS traffic with NetworkPolicy?
faq.answer: Create an egress rule allowing UDP port 53 to the kube-system namespace where DNS pods typically run.

faq.question: Can I block access to cloud metadata endpoints?
faq.answer: Yes, use NetworkPolicy egress rules with ipBlock.except to block 169.254.169.254/32, preventing pods from accessing AWS/Azure metadata services.
